source_metadata.title_raw: Aaron Harp - A-19-040 - April 17,2019 - Newport Beach

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

April 15, 2019

Aaron Harp
City Attorney
City of Newport Beach
100 Civic Center Drive
Newport Beach, CA 92660

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-19-040

Dear Mr. Harp:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Newport Beach City
Councilmember Will O'Neill regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform
Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest
provisions of the Act and not under any other conflict of interest laws. Also note that we are not a
finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we
provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts
underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTION

Does the Act prohibit Newport Beach City Councilmember Will O'Neill from taking part in
Newport Beach's governmental decisions relating to a potential partnership with the City of Costa
Mesa regarding Costa Mesa's project to establish and operate a "permanent bridge homeless
shelter," in which Newport Beach would provide financial consideration in exchange for a portion
of the project's shelter capacity, given that the Councilmember is a non-equity partner in a law firm
that has a long-term lease for its offices located approximately 750 feet from the project site?

CONCLUSION

No. The Act does not prohibit the Councilmember from taking part in decisions relating to a
potential partnership with Costa Mesa regarding the project because it is not reasonably foreseeable
that those decisions would have a material financial effect on the Councilmember's financial
interests in the law firm or his personal finances and those of immediate family members.

___________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-19-040
Page No. 2

FACTS

On March 6, 2019, the Costa Mesa City Council approved Costa Mesa's purchase of real
property located at 3175 Airway Avenue in Costa Mesa (the "Project Site") in order to establish and
operate a 50-bed "permanent bridge homeless shelter" (the "Project") on that property. According
to a Costa Mesa Staff Report relating to the Project dated January 10, 2019, a "bridge homeless
shelter" is a facility that provides both shelter and human services to the homeless.

According to a Costa Mesa Staff Report recommending that the Costa Mesa City Council
approve the acquisition of the Project Site dated March 1, 2019: the Project Site is a 1.34-acre
parcel of real property located in Costa Mesa just west of John Wayne Airport; there is an existing
29,816 square foot industrial warehouse located on the Project Site, which includes 5,510 square
feet of built-out office space with lobby and reception areas, private offices, storage areas, a
breakroom, and restrooms; and Costa Mesa's acquisition costs for the Project Site are projected to
total $6,925,000.

You are the Newport Beach City Attorney and the authorized representative of Newport
Beach City Councilmember Will O'Neill. You state that Newport Beach is interested in discussing
the Project with Costa Mesa, and that the Newport Beach City Council may soon consider its own
governmental decisions related to the Project. You provided additional information on March 25,
2019, advising that the decisions at issue relate to Newport Beach's potential pursuit of a
partnership with Costa Mesa in which Newport Beach would provide financial consideration in
exchange for a portion of the Project's shelter capacity. You also advised that those decisions do not
relate to limiting, preventing, or relocating the Project.

In his private capacity, the Councilmember is a non-equity partner in the law firm Ross,
Werching, & Wolcott, LLP ("RWW"). According to its website, RWW represents companies and
select individuals in a variety of business, real estate, and litigation matters, and its clients include
private 500 and other publicly traded companies, international manufacturers, and corporate
executives. RWW has a long-term lease for its offices located in Building S at 3151 Airway Avenue
in Costa Mesa, and those offices are located approximately 750 feet southwest of the Project Site.
You state that the Councilmember has not engaged in actions related to the Project to date.

ANALYSES

The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit a public official from taking part in a
governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision would have a material
financial effect on one or more of the official's financial interests. (Sections 87100 and 87103.) An
official's interests that may give rise to a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act are
identified in Section 87103. With respect to Newport Beach's decisions relating to the Project, the
Councilmember has a business interest in RWW because he is an employee of RWW (see Section
87103(d)), a source of income interest in RWW assuming he has received $500 or more in income
from RWW in the previous 12 months (see Section 87103(c)), and an interest in his personal
finances and those of immediate family members (see Section 87103).²

² Section 82033 sets forth the Act's definition of an "interest in real property," and provides that an
individual's interests in real property include "a pro rata share of interests in real property of any business entity or trust

File No. A-19-040
Page No. 3

Foreseeability and Materiality

Regulation 18701(a) provides that a decision's financial effect on an official's financial interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the official's interest is "explicitly involved" in the decision; an official's interest is "explicitly involved" if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, the decision; and an interest is the "subject of a proceeding" if the decision at issue involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the interest. Regulation 18701(b) sets forth the foreseeability standard applicable to a decision's effect on an official's interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision, and provides that the decision's effect on such an interest is reasonably foreseeable if it "can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical."

RWW is not explicitly involved in Newport Beach's potential decisions relating to the Project. Therefore, those decisions' effect on RWW is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical.

Regulation 18702.1(b) provides that a decision's effect on an official's interest in a privately-held business entity is material if a prudent person with sufficient information would find that it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision's effect would contribute to a change in the value of the business.³ Regulation 18702.1(b) also provides examples of types of decisions that may have a material effect on a business entity under this materiality standard, including those that:

(1) Authorize, prohibit, regulate or otherwise establish conditions for an activity in which the business entity is engaged;
(2) Increase or decrease the amount of competition in the field in which the business entity is engaged;
(3) Increase or decrease the need for the products or services that the business entity supplies;
(4) Make improvements in the surrounding neighborhood such as redevelopment projects, traffic/road improvements, or parking changes that may affect, either temporarily or permanently, the amount of business the business entity receives;

_____________________________________________________________________________________________________

in which the individual or immediate family owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially, a 10-percent interest or greater." You state that the Councilmember is a "non-equity partner" in RWW. The precise meaning of that term of art, however, can differ from law firm to law firm. Therefore, we note that the Councilmember would also have a real property interest in his pro rata share of RWW's real property interests, including its leasehold interest in its offices, if he has a 10-percent or greater interest in RWW.

³ Regulation 18702.3 sets forth the materiality standards applicable to a decision's effect on an official's source of income interest, and subdivision (a)(4) of that regulation provides that if the income is from the sale of goods or services and if the source of income is a business entity, materiality is determined pursuant to Regulation 18702.1. Regulation 18702.5 sets forth the materiality standard applicable to a decision's effect on an official's interest in his or her personal finances and those of immediate family members, and subdivision (c) of that regulation provides that if the decision also affects the official's business interest, materiality is determined pursuant to Regulation 18702.1. Therefore, we apply Regulation 18702.1 to determine if Newport Beach's decisions relating to the Project would have a disqualifying effect on the Councilmember's interests at issue.

File No. A-19-040
Page No. 4

(5) Decide the location of a major development, entertainment facility, or
other project that would increase or decrease the amount of business the entity
draws from the location of the project;
(6) Increase or decrease the tax burden, debt, or financial or legal liability
of the business entity.

Thus, at issue is whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the effect of Newport Beach's decisions
relating to a potential partnership between Newport Beach and Costa Mesa regarding the Project
would contribute to a change in the value of RWW.

The Project would establish a 50-bed permanent bridge homeless shelter on the 1.34-acre
Project Site which would provide shelter and human services to the homeless. Although RWW
leases its offices pursuant to a long-term lease, and those offices are within approximately 750 feet
of the Project Site, the decisions at issue do not relate to limiting, preventing, or relocating the
Project. You state that those decisions solely relate to a potential partnership between Newport
Beach and Costa Mesa regarding the Project in which Newport Beach would provide financial
consideration to Costa Mesa in exchange for a portion of the Project's shelter capacity. Decisions
relating to the potential partnership are not of a type listed in the examples of types of decisions that
may have a material effect under Regulation 18702.1(b). And there is no indication from the facts
presented that Costa Mesa's decisions to go forward with the Project will be dependent on the
establishment of a potential partnership with Newport Beach or that Newport Beach's decisions
relating to the potential partnership will lead to an expansion of the Project or its future operations.4

Therefore, based on the facts presented, it is not reasonably foreseeable that decisions
relating to the potential partnership between Newport Beach and Costa Mesa regarding the Project
would contribute to a change in the value of RWW. Accordingly, the Act does not prohibit the
Councilmember from taking part in those decisions.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

[illegible]

By: Matthew F. Christy
Counsel, Legal Division

MFC:aja

___________________________
4 If Newport Beach's decisions relating to the Project do not solely relate to the potential partnership between
Newport Beach and Costa Mesa regarding the Project, or if there is any indication that those decisions may result in the
expansion, limitation, prevention, or relocation of the Project, you should seek additional advice.

sections.question:
Does the Act prohibit Newport Beach City Councilmember Will O'Neill from taking part in 
Newport Beach's governmental decisions relating to a potential partnership with the City of Costa 
Mesa regarding Costa Mesa's project to establish and operate a "permanent bridge homeless 
shelter," in which Newport Beach would provide financial consideration in exchange for a portion 
of the project's shelter capacity, given that the Councilmember is a non-equity partner in a law firm 
that has a long-term lease for its offices located approximately 750 feet from the project site?
sections.conclusion:
No. The Act does not prohibit the Councilmember from taking part in decisions relating to a 
potential partnership with Costa Mesa regarding the project because it is not reasonably foreseeable 
that those decisions would have a material financial effect on the Councilmember's financial 
interests in the law firm or his personal finances and those of immediate family members.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does the Act prohibit Newport Beach City Councilmember Will O'Neill from taking part in 
Newport Beach's governmental decisions relating to a potential partnership with the City of Costa 
Mesa regarding Costa Mesa's project to establish and operate a "permanent bridge homeless 
shelter," in which Newport Beach would provide financial consideration in exchange for a portion 
of the project's shelter capacity, given that the Councilmember is a non-equity partner in a law firm 
that has a long-term lease for its offices located approximately 750 feet from the project site?

CONCLUSION: No. The Act does not prohibit the Councilmember from taking part in decisions relating to a 
potential partnership with Costa Mesa regarding the project because it is not reasonably foreseeable 
that those decisions would have a material financial effect on the Councilmember's financial 
interests in the law firm or his personal finances and those of immediate family members.
